common_name: Black Magic Elephant Ear (Dark Purple-Black Giant Colocasia)

planting.note: Plant tubers or divisions in spring after the last frost when soil has warmed to 60°F in moist to wet, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; in zone 9, tubers can be left in the ground year-round with light mulch protection — they do not need to be lifted for winter storage.

directions: Plant in moist to wet, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to partial sun; Black Magic is the most celebrated dark-leaved elephant ear cultivar — producing enormous, heart-shaped leaves (up to 30 inches long on established plants) in a uniform, deep burgundy-purple to nearly black color that is unique in the world of large-foliage tropical plants. The consistent, very dark coloration — maintained throughout the growing season without fading to green — creates a dramatic, almost surreal visual impact that makes a single established plant an unmistakable focal point in any garden. The contrast between the near-black leaves and companion plants of lighter color (chartreuse foliage, bright flowers, white flowers) is particularly striking. Black Magic thrives in the heat, humidity, and heavy rainfall of the Gulf South summer — it grows most vigorously in July and August in zone 9, producing new leaves at an impressive rate under optimal conditions. The plant does equally well in standing water (pond margins, boggy areas) as in consistently moist garden soil — its natural habitat includes flooded rice paddies and water garden margins. In zone 9, Black Magic is perennial, dying back in cold winters but reliably re-emerging from the persistent tubers in spring. Mass plantings create a dramatic, dark tropical ground layer beneath light-colored structures or light-foliaged trees.

planting.note: Plant tubers or divisions in spring after the last frost in moist to wet, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to partial sun; Illustris produces its most vivid dark coloration in bright partial shade — deep shade reduces the intensity of the dark patterning while full sun may stress plants in extreme Gulf South summer heat.

directions: Plant in moist to wet, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to partial sun; Illustris (also called Imperial Taro) is a patterned dark elephant ear producing large, heart-shaped leaves in a distinctive two-tone pattern of dark blackish-purple or dark green-black leaf tissue veined and sectioned with contrasting lighter green veins — creating an intricate, stained-glass effect on each leaf. The veining pattern varies from leaf to leaf but consistently creates an effect of dark panels separated by lighter green margins along each major vein, giving the leaf a sectioned, almost geometric appearance when viewed from above or in backlight. This patterned leaf effect is more complex and visually interesting than the uniform dark color of 'Black Magic' — Illustris provides textural detail and pattern rather than uniform dark mass. Like all Colocasia esculenta cultivars, Illustris thrives in the Gulf South's warm, humid summer conditions — growing most vigorously in July and August and tolerating the heat and humidity that would stress many other large-foliage plants. The plant spreads by offsets to form expanding clumps over time — a self-maintaining quality in zone 9 gardens where the tubers persist year-round. The large dark leaves and intricate veining create an outstanding tropical accent that pairs beautifully with bright flowers and light-colored architectural features.

common_name: Candidum Caladium (Classic Green-Veined White Fancy Caladium)

planting.note: Plant tubers in spring after the last frost date when soil has warmed to at least 65°F; plant 1–2 inches deep in moist, well-drained, humus-rich, acidic (pH 5.5–6.5) soil in partial to full shade; Candidum has been in cultivation since the 1860s and is the prototype white fancy caladium from which many later white cultivars were derived.

directions: Plant tubers in spring after the last frost in moist, well-drained, humus-rich, acidic soil in partial to full shade; Candidum is the oldest and most historically significant white fancy caladium cultivar — first listed in 1867 and still one of the most widely grown caladiums in the world after more than 150 years of continuous cultivation. The large, heart-shaped leaves are pure white with prominent, contrasting deep-green veins radiating from the central midrib and branching through the white leaf tissue in a clean, bold pattern that is one of the most graphic and visually striking leaf patterns in the plant kingdom. The bright white leaf with crisp green veining creates a unique foliage display that can brighten a deep shade position as effectively as a light fixture — the luminous white reflects available light and makes dark areas appear significantly brighter. Candidum's longevity in cultivation reflects its extraordinary reliability and quality as both a garden plant and a cut-foliage crop (the leaves are used in floral arrangements). The standard white-veined caladium used as a benchmark for all subsequent white varieties, Candidum remains a top choice because no later introduction has consistently surpassed it in the combination of large leaf size, clean white color, bold green veining, and reliable performance in hot, humid Gulf South conditions. A true classic of American horticulture.

planting.note: Plant tubers in spring after the last frost date when soil has warmed to at least 65°F; plant 1–2 inches deep in moist, well-drained, humus-rich, acidic soil in partial shade; Gingerland is a strap-leaved type caladium — the leaf shape is more elongated and narrower than standard heart-shaped fancy types, creating a different textural effect.

directions: Plant tubers in spring after the last frost in moist, well-drained, humus-rich, acidic soil in partial shade; Gingerland is one of the most popular strap-leaf caladium cultivars — producing narrower, more elongated, lance-shaped leaves (rather than the broad heart shape of standard fancy caladiums) in a pattern of creamy-white with scattered red-crimson speckling and a green margin that creates a soft, delicate appearance quite different from the bold leaf patterns of large-leaved types. The strap-leaf form produces a more graceful, airy texture in mass plantings compared to the bold, overlapping effect of large fancy caladiums — Gingerland creates a lighter, more refined textural effect appropriate for intimate garden spaces and detailed planting designs. The fine red spotting on the white leaf surface becomes more vivid in brighter conditions, making Gingerland a versatile cultivar that can be used in bright indirect light to partial shade. Strap-leaf types like Gingerland typically tolerate somewhat more sun than standard fancy types and are often recommended for partial-sun locations (3–4 hours direct sun) where their coloration remains good without the bleaching that affects white-leaved fancy cultivars. In mixed caladium plantings, Gingerland's finer texture creates effective contrast with the large, bold leaves of 'Candidum', 'White Queen', or 'Red Flash'.

common_name: Lemon Drop Pepper (Fruity Lemon-Yellow Peruvian Hot Pepper)

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 10–12 weeks before transplanting (C. chinense is slower to germinate and develop than C. annuum); transplant into fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in full sun after the last frost; Lemon Drop requires a long warm season — in zone 9, the long growing season supports full production.

directions: Set out transplants in fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in full sun after the last frost date; Lemon Drop is a traditional Peruvian pepper (Ají Lemon in Peru) — a small (1–1.5 inch), thin-walled, conical fruit with a distinctive bright-lemon-yellow color at maturity and a unique combination of intense heat (15,000–30,000 Scoville units — comparable to a serrano but with more heat variability) and fruity, distinctly citrus-lemon flavor. The characteristic lemon flavor — a fruity, citrus-forward quality quite unlike the neutral heat of jalapeños or the fruity sweetness of habaneros — makes Lemon Drop one of the most flavorful hot peppers for cooking applications where heat AND flavor complexity are desired simultaneously. The dried and powdered fruit has an exceptional flavor that is widely used in traditional Peruvian cuisine for adding heat and bright citrus notes. As a Capsicum chinense species, Lemon Drop requires somewhat higher temperatures for germination (80–90°F) and a longer season than C. annuum peppers — start seeds 10–12 weeks before transplanting for adequate development time. The plant is productive in zone 9's long warm season, producing prolifically from late spring through frost. Fresh fruits, dried fruits, and powder form are all excellent culinary applications. The vivid yellow color and intense fruity aroma make Lemon Drop a distinctive addition to the pepper garden.
